sanskrit_devanagari:
यं सन्न्यासमिति प्राहुर्योगं तं विद्धि पाण्डव ।
न ह्यसन्न्यस्तसङ्कल्पो योगी भवति कश्चन ॥ २ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
yaṁ sannyāsam iti prāhur yogaṁ taṁ viddhi pāṇḍava |
na hy asannyasta-saṅkalpo yogī bhavati kaścana || 2 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: What is called renunciation you should know to be the same as yoga, or linking oneself with the Supreme, O son of Pāṇḍu, for one can never become a yogī unless he renounces the desire for sense gratification.

What is called renunciation you should know to be the same as yoga, or linking oneself with the Supreme, O son of Pandu, for one can never become a yogi unless he renounces the desire for sense gratification.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1549 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Real sannyāsa-yoga or bhakti means that one should know his constitutional position as the living entity, and act accordingly. The living entity has no separate independent identity. He is the marginal energy of the Supreme.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.2 Yam, that which is characterized by the giving up of all actions and their results; which prahuh, they, the knowers of the Vedas and the Smrtis, call; sannyasam iti, monasticism, in the real sense; viddhi, known; tam, that monasticism in the real sense; to be yogam, Yoga, consisting in…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-2-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: 6.2 What they speak of as Sannyasa, that, know, O Pandava, to be Yoga; for none becomes a Yogin without renouncing desires (sankalpa). Sankalpas — the imaginings born of desire about ends — are at the root of all karmas; their renunciation is the renunciation of all karmas in their root.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.2 Know Karma Yoga only to be that which they call as Sannyasa i.e., as Jnana Yoga or knowledge of the real nature of the self. Sri Krsna substantiates this by the words, ‘For no one whose delusive identification of the body with the self is not abandoned, becomes a true Karma Yogin.’ ‘On…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-2-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: 6.2 Yoga and Sannyasa are not different. The renunciation of fruits of action is karma-yoga; that very renunciation, when extended to the saṅkalpa or motivating intention itself, is sannyāsa. No one truly becomes a yogi without renouncing the saṅkalpas.

positions.rendering: the imaginings born of desire about ends; their renunciation is the renunciation of all karmas in their root
the motivating intention behind the action — the saṅkalpa-of-fruit-desire

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Shankara reads saṅkalpa as the desire-laden imaginings that motivate karma — its renunciation is structural (root cut). Ramanuja narrows it to the motivating intention of fruit-seeking. Prabhupada renders it more devotionally as desire for sense-gratification. The operational instruction (cut at intention, not at action) is identical across readings.

literal_meaning: What they call sannyāsa, know that to be yoga, O Pāṇḍava — for no one becomes a yogi without renouncing fancies (saṅkalpa).
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna sharpens the equation. What is called renunciation IS what is called yoga; the two are one teaching. The cut is at saṅkalpa — the desire-laden imaginings about results — not at action itself. Without giving up these saṅkalpa-fancies no one becomes a yogi. The doctrine clarifies that what 6.1 collapsed was not the difference between two doctrinal labels but a real identity-claim about the inner orientation.
